conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, let me ask: **Do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children. I have two - Alex, who is 6 years old, and Blake, who's 3. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have two children - Alex (6 years old) and Blake (3 years old).

Before I can assess your eligibility, let me gather some important information by reviewing the official rules.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I have the information I need about the eligibility rules. Let me ask you some questions to assess your situation.

**Do both Alex and Blake live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live with me here in the UK. Well, actually... Blake has been in hospital for a few months now, about 3 months or so. But Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I'm sorry to hear Blake has been in hospital.

To help me assess Blake's situation properly, I need to ask: **During the time Blake has been in hospital (approximately 3 months), have you been regularly spending money on them - for example, on clothes, toys, treats, or other items for Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on their needs while they're in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand that can be a difficult situation.

Now, to complete my assessment, I need to ask a few more questions:

**Are you responsible for both children - meaning no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just one more question to complete my assessment:

**Do you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000 per year?**

(This won't affect your eligibility to receive Child Benefit, but it may mean you need to pay a tax charge.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us earns over £60,000 a year.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake based on the rules.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, here is the outcome for your Child Benefit eligibility:

**For Alex (6 years old): ✓ Eligible**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them with no one else claiming. You meet all the standard criteria.

**For Blake (3 years old): ✗ Not Eligible**
Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time. While Blake is under 16 and you are responsible for them, Blake has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (12 weeks or more). Under the rules for children in hospital, Child Benefit entitlement typically ends after 12 weeks unless you regularly spend money on them. Since you've indicated you haven't been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while they're in hospital, this exception does not apply.

**Important note:** If Blake returns home from hospital, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for them again. Also, if your circumstances change and you're able to start regularly spending money on Blake while they're in hospital, you should contact HMRC as this may affect your entitlement.

Since neither you nor your partner earn over £60,000, you won't need to worry about the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about your Child Benefit eligibility?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK, and you'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Alex meets all these criteria, so you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old (under 16) and you are responsible for them, with no one else claiming Child Benefit. However, Blake has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (12 weeks or more). According to the rules for children in hospital, after 12 weeks in hospital, you're usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless there are exceptions - such as if you regularly spend money on them. You have confirmed that you have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while they're in hospital. Therefore, the exception does not apply, and you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake after the 12-week period in hospital.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (6) and Blake (3). They live in the UK and are responsible for both children with no one else claiming. Alex lives at home with them and meets all standard eligibility criteria. Blake has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks), and the claimant has not been regularly spending money on Blake during this time. Under the hospital rules, Child Benefit entitlement typically ends after 12 weeks unless the claimant regularly spends money on the child, which is not the case here. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex but not for Blake.
